text.heading: These troublesome 76 oil wells
text.p: A Pandora box was opened in August last year when the Revenue Mobilisation, Allocation and Fiscal Commission (RMAFC) went for a retreat in Kano and ‘resolved’ the maritime boundary issue between Akwa Ibom and Cross River States. At the end of the meeting, the disputed maritime territory was resolved in favour of Akwa Ibom State. Consequently, 76 oil wells hitherto belonging to Cross River State were ceded to Akwa Ibom, leaving the former virtually without oil well, the implication of which is that Cross River was struck off the list of the prestigious club of oil producing states.

One might ask: what is the big deal about being an oil producing state, what about other states that are not producing oil? The problem is that first, non oil producing states do not have expectation of derivation income from the Federation Account and so do not plan big. Secondly, they are not used to large income and so are never big spenders. States like Delta, Rivers, Bayelsa, Akwa Ibom and Cross River are big earners and big spenders. By virtue of their income, they have acquired a large appetite and capacity for spending. This is why the delisting of Cross River from the league of oil producing states is bound to lead to serious financial, social and security crisis in the state. The corollary to this is the way Benin Republic and Niger are not likely to be distressed over falling oil prices since they are not oil exporters.

For a state collecting N5 billion, N6 billion or N8 billion from the Federation Account in the past, it would have employed workers commensurate with its income. It also would have embarked on projects along this line. It is not unlikely too that it had enlisted militants and idle local chiefs on its payroll. The cessation of funds and reduction of income to a paltry N1 billion meant that contractors would no longer be paid, workers would have to be retrenched, and even the lifeline to the militants cut off. It is a recipe for economic and social chaos.

All these happened as fallout of the ceding of Bakassi to Cameroon. Nearly all the oil wells belonging to the two states are actually offshore, as part of Nigerian territorial waters. It is interesting to note that Akwa Ibom in particular has no onshore oil wells.

The two states, indeed the entire Niger Delta States, were thrown into apprehension when late Chief Bola Ige as the then Attorney General and Minister of Justice, went to the Supreme Court to affirm the Federal Government’s exclusive right to the offshore oil wells. The court concurred by making the low watermark to be the seaward boundary of the states, cutting the two states off from derivation. But wisdom prevailed and a political solution was found which extended the seaward boundary by 200 metres isobath from the low watermark of the littoral states and practically dumped the Supreme Court’s judgement. This is why Akwa Ibom gets as much as N16 billion from the Federation Account.

Cross River’s problem is that the ceding of Bakassi, which was part of her territory, to Cameroon, redrew the map of the two states. Since her offshore oil extends from Bakassi, the loss of that territory should imply the loss of the offshore oil wells. But there is a cruel twist of geography here. What the ICJ gave Cameroon was the land and not the oil wells which still belong to Nigeria. However, with Bakassi out of Cross River, the oil wells now abut Akwa Ibom and this is what RMAFC quickly adjusted to and gave the oil wells to the later.

Nevertheless, the matter is not that straightforward. Redrawing maritime boundary is problematic and when it is within an economic zone, it is a sure conflict generator. That is why we are where we are at the moment. Cross River, the loser in this maritime mathematics, had her revenue reduced to less that N1 billion and has gone to court. It has also appealed to the Presidency.

When this kind of conflict arises, the parties had the options of looking at the matter strictly from a technical point. It could also take the benefit of history or adopt a political solution. The technical option is the reliance on equidistance and strict cartography by drawing a straight line between two points. This option will appear superfluous at the moment since the conflict emerged from this cartography. The historical option can be of help. One, the oil wells have always belonged to Cross Rivers. Indeed, they were the subject of boundary dispute between the two states before the Bakassi imbroglio. When the dispute could not be settled through technicalities, the National Boundaries Commission (NBC) had to rely on history titles and awarded 76 oil wells, out of the disputed 80, to Cross River. As recommended by ONCLOS 82, there should be recourse to equidistance method in resolving maritime boundary dispute only when there is no bilateral agreement, historical titles and special circumstances.

One implication of relying on technicality is that RMAFC would have redrawn the boundary between Cross River and Akwa Ibom. This would appear illegal since it will take an act of the National Assembly to do so.

I must confess that there is no easy solution. From the way the issue is engaged, one state’s gain would be another’s loss. Technicality gives it to Akwa Ibom while historical title says the oil wells belong to Cross River. There must be a third way out which is the political solution. In the balance of probabilities, even when the oil wells are returned to Cross Rivers, Akwa Ibom will still be in a good stead. Although it is not before us what a state earns, ceding the oil wells to Cross River will not impoverish Akwa Ibom.

I will suggest that the matter be settled out of court and a middle way be found such that most, if not all, the oil wells are returned to Cross River State. It is in the spirit of good neighbourliness. The Presidency should broker this deal. RMAFC should also learn to thread carefully in the future and consider the political and economic circumstances before taking a precipitate action.

The dispute over the oil wells is an opportunity for all the oil producing states, including Cross River, to factor in the finite nature of a God-given resource and widen the base of their economy. Anything can happen tomorrow that will leave you high and dry. This lesson should be learnt by Nigeria, more than anyone.
